item.title: Chapter 4: Time Series Analysis
item.dc:creator: Pawan Jangra
item.description:
Learning Objectives: Decompose time series into trend, seasonality, and noise. Understand autocorrelation, fit ARIMA models, and produce forecasts with uncertainty bounds.

1 What is a Time Series?

A time series is a sequence of observations indexed by time. Unlike cross-sectional data, observations are not independent — past values influence future ones.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Simulate monthly employment data (2015-2024)
set.seed(2025)
n_months <- 120  # 10 years
time_index <- seq(as.Date("2015-01-01"), by="month", length.out=n_months)

employment <- data.frame(
  date = time_index,
  month_num = 1:n_months
) |>
  mutate(
    trend     = 100 + 0.15 * month_num + 0.0005 * month_num^2,
    seasonal  = 3 * sin(2 * pi * month_num / 12) + 
                1.5 * cos(2 * pi * month_num / 6),
    noise     = cumsum(rnorm(n_months, 0, 0.8)),
    employment = trend + seasonal + noise
  )

2 Visualising Time Series

Code 
ggplot(employment, aes(x=date, y=employment)) +
  geom_line(color="#2563eb", linewidth=0.9) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", span=0.3, color="#e11d48", 
              se=FALSE, linetype="dashed", linewidth=1) +
  labs(title="Monthly Employment Index (2015–2024)",
       subtitle="Blue = observed data · Red dashed = LOESS trend",
       x=NULL, y="Employment Index") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

3 Decomposition

We can decompose a time series into its components:

Code 
ts_data <- ts(employment$employment, start=c(2015,1), frequency=12)
decomp <- decompose(ts_data, type="additive")

# Convert to tidy format for ggplot
decomp_df <- data.frame(
  date     = time_index,
  Observed = as.numeric(decomp$x),
  Trend    = as.numeric(decomp$trend),
  Seasonal = as.numeric(decomp$seasonal),
  Random   = as.numeric(decomp$random)
) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to="Component", values_to="Value") |>
  mutate(Component = factor(Component, 
    levels=c("Observed","Trend","Seasonal","Random")))

ggplot(decomp_df, aes(x=date, y=Value)) +
  geom_line(color="#2563eb", linewidth=0.7, na.rm=TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~Component, ncol=1, scales="free_y") +
  labs(title="Time Series Decomposition",
       subtitle="Additive model: Y = Trend + Seasonal + Random",
       x=NULL, y=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        strip.text=element_text(face="bold"))

4 Autocorrelation

Autocorrelation measures how correlated a time series is with its past values (lags).

Code 
acf_vals  <- acf(ts_data, lag.max=36, plot=FALSE)
pacf_vals <- pacf(ts_data, lag.max=36, plot=FALSE)

acf_df <- data.frame(
  lag = as.numeric(acf_vals$lag),
  acf = as.numeric(acf_vals$acf),
  type = "ACF"
)

pacf_df <- data.frame(
  lag = as.numeric(pacf_vals$lag),
  acf = as.numeric(pacf_vals$acf),
  type = "PACF"
)

ci_val <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(length(ts_data))

bind_rows(acf_df, pacf_df) |>
  filter(lag > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=lag, y=acf)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0, color="#64748b") +
  geom_hline(yintercept=c(ci_val,-ci_val), linetype="dashed", 
             color="#e11d48", alpha=0.7) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend=lag, yend=0), color="#2563eb", linewidth=1) +
  geom_point(color="#2563eb", size=2) +
  facet_wrap(~type, ncol=1) +
  labs(title="Autocorrelation (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation (PACF)",
       subtitle="Red dashed lines = 95% confidence bounds",
       x="Lag (months)", y="Correlation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(), strip.text=element_text(face="bold"))

Reading ACF/PACF: - Slowly decaying ACF → non-stationary series, needs differencing - ACF cuts off after lag q → MA(q) component - PACF cuts off after lag p → AR(p) component

5 Simple Forecasting: Moving Averages

Code 
employment <- employment |>
  mutate(
    ma3  = zoo::rollmean(employment, 3,  fill=NA, align="right"),
    ma12 = zoo::rollmean(employment, 12, fill=NA, align="right")
  )

ggplot(employment, aes(x=date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y=employment, color="Observed"), alpha=0.6) +
  geom_line(aes(y=ma3,  color="3-month MA"), linewidth=1) +
  geom_line(aes(y=ma12, color="12-month MA"), linewidth=1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values=c("Observed"="#94a3b8","3-month MA"="#7c3aed","12-month MA"="#e11d48"),
    name=""
  ) +
  labs(title="Employment Index with Moving Averages",
       x=NULL, y="Employment Index") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="top", panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

6 Simple Trend Forecast

Code 
# Fit linear trend model
trend_model <- lm(employment ~ month_num + I(month_num^2), data=employment)

# Forecast next 24 months
future <- data.frame(
  date      = seq(max(employment$date) %m+% months(1), 
                  by="month", length.out=24),
  month_num = (n_months+1):(n_months+24)
)
future$forecast <- predict(trend_model, newdata=future)
future$se       <- predict(trend_model, newdata=future, se.fit=TRUE)$se.fit

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data=future, 
              aes(x=date, ymin=forecast-2*se, ymax=forecast+2*se),
              fill="#93c5fd", alpha=0.4) +
  geom_line(data=employment, aes(x=date, y=employment), 
            color="#2563eb", linewidth=0.8) +
  geom_line(data=future, aes(x=date, y=forecast), 
            color="#e11d48", linewidth=1.1, linetype="dashed") +
  labs(title="Employment Trend Forecast (2025–2026)",
       subtitle="Blue = historical · Red dashed = forecast · Shaded = 95% CI",
       x=NULL, y="Employment Index") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

7 Stationarity Testing

Code 
# Augmented Dickey-Fuller test (manual approximation for display)
# In practice: use tseries::adf.test()

cat("Stationarity Assessment:\n")
Stationarity Assessment:
Code 
cat("━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━\n")
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Code 
cat("Visual inspection: Upward trend observed → likely non-stationary\n")
Visual inspection: Upward trend observed → likely non-stationary
Code 
cat("ACF: Slowly decaying → confirms non-stationarity\n")
ACF: Slowly decaying → confirms non-stationarity
Code 
cat("\nRecommendation: Apply first differencing\n")

Recommendation: Apply first differencing
Code 
diff_data <- diff(ts_data)
cat("\nAfter first differencing:\n")

After first differencing:
Code 
cat("Mean:", round(mean(diff_data), 4), "(≈0 → good)\n")
Mean: 0.1775 (≈0 → good)
Code 
cat("Variance appears constant → stationary ✓\n")
Variance appears constant → stationary ✓

8 Key Concepts Summary

Concept Description R Function
Decomposition Trend + Seasonal + Random decompose()
ACF Autocorrelation function acf()
PACF Partial autocorrelation pacf()
ADF Test Stationarity test tseries::adf.test()
ARIMA Auto-regressive model arima(), forecast::auto.arima()
Moving Average Smoothing zoo::rollmean()

9 Practice Exercises

TipTry It Yourself
  1. Use forecast::auto.arima() to automatically select the best ARIMA model
  2. Compute and plot exponential smoothing with HoltWinters()
  3. Formally test for stationarity with tseries::adf.test()
  4. Forecast the next 12 months and evaluate accuracy using MAPE
NoteWhat’s Next?

This chapter covered the fundamentals. For advanced time series work with R, explore: - forecast package — comprehensive forecasting tools - fable package — tidy time series forecasting - tsibble package — tidy temporal data structures - Vector Autoregression (VAR) — for multivariate time series

item.title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
item.dc:creator: Pawan Jangra
item.description:
Learning Objectives: Fit and interpret simple and multiple linear regression models, check OLS assumptions through diagnostic plots, and communicate results clearly.

1 What is Regression?

Regression analysis quantifies the relationship between variables. The goal is to model how changes in explanatory variables (X) are associated with changes in the outcome variable (Y).

The linear regression model:

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(broom)

# Wage dataset with labour economics flavor
set.seed(2025)
n <- 800
wage_data <- data.frame(
  edu_years = sample(0:20, n, replace=TRUE, prob=dpois(0:20, 9)+0.01),
  experience = sample(0:40, n, replace=TRUE),
  female = rbinom(n, 1, 0.4),
  urban = rbinom(n, 1, 0.55),
  union = rbinom(n, 1, 0.18)
) |>
  mutate(
    log_wage = 4.5 + 0.08*edu_years + 0.04*experience - 0.003*experience^2
               - 0.25*female + 0.15*urban + 0.12*union + rnorm(n, 0, 0.35),
    wage = round(exp(log_wage))
  )

2 Simple Linear Regression

Let’s first explore the education-wage relationship:

Code 
# Scatter with regression line
ggplot(wage_data, aes(x=edu_years, y=wage)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.3, color="#2563eb", size=1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#e11d48", se=TRUE, linewidth=1.2,
              fill="#fca5a5", alpha=0.3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::label_number(prefix="₹")) +
  labs(title="Years of Education vs Daily Wage",
       subtitle="OLS regression line with 95% confidence band",
       x="Years of Education", y="Daily Wage (₹)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())
Code 
# Fit simple linear regression
model_simple <- lm(wage ~ edu_years, data=wage_data)
tidy(model_simple) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 3)) |>
  kable(caption="Simple Linear Regression: Wage ~ Education")
Simple Linear Regression: Wage ~ Education
term estimate std.error statistic p.value
(Intercept) 34.985 10.237 3.417 0.001
edu_years 11.321 1.031 10.980 0.000
Code 
glance(model_simple) |>
  select(r.squared, adj.r.squared, sigma, statistic, p.value, nobs) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 4)) |>
  kable(caption="Model fit statistics")
Model fit statistics
r.squared adj.r.squared sigma statistic p.value nobs
0.1313 0.1302 110.7371 120.5613 0 800

Interpretation: For each additional year of education, daily wages increase by ₹11 on average, holding all else constant.

3 Multiple Linear Regression

Now let’s add more variables:

Code 
model_full <- lm(wage ~ edu_years + experience + I(experience^2) + 
                   female + urban + union, data=wage_data)

tidy(model_full) |>
  mutate(
    term = recode(term,
      `(Intercept)`="Intercept",
      edu_years="Education (years)",
      experience="Experience (years)",
      `I(experience^2)`="Experience² (quadratic)",
      female="Female (=1)",
      urban="Urban (=1)",
      union="Union member (=1)"
    ),
    across(where(is.numeric), round, 4),
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "**",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "*",
      TRUE            ~ ""
    )
  ) |>
  kable(caption="Multiple Regression Results (*** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05)")
Multiple Regression Results (*** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05)
term estimate std.error statistic p.value significance
Intercept 136.9419 9.6012 14.2629 0.0000 ***
Education (years) 11.0924 0.6676 16.6161 0.0000 ***
Experience (years) -2.4950 0.8015 -3.1131 0.0019 **
Experience² (quadratic) -0.1063 0.0196 -5.4143 0.0000 ***
Female (=1) -30.0160 5.2114 -5.7597 0.0000 ***
Urban (=1) 15.0396 5.1328 2.9301 0.0035 **
Union member (=1) 38.7287 7.0472 5.4956 0.0000 ***
Code 
# Compare models
bind_rows(
  glance(model_simple) |> mutate(Model="Simple (Education only)"),
  glance(model_full)   |> mutate(Model="Full Model")
) |>
  select(Model, r.squared, adj.r.squared, sigma, AIC) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 3)) |>
  kable(caption="Model comparison")
Model comparison
Model r.squared adj.r.squared sigma AIC
Simple (Education only) 0.131 0.130 110.737 9805.753
Full Model 0.639 0.637 71.572 9112.400

4 Regression Diagnostics

Always check the four core OLS assumptions:

Code 
par(mfrow=c(2,2), mar=c(4,4,3,1), family="sans")
plot(model_full, which=1:4, 
     col=alpha("#2563eb", 0.4), pch=20, cex=0.7,
     col.smooth="#e11d48", lwd.smooth=2)
Code 
par(mfrow=c(1,1))

What to look for: - Residuals vs Fitted: Should show no pattern (random scatter around 0) - Q-Q Plot: Points should follow the diagonal line (normality of residuals) - Scale-Location: Horizontal band (homoscedasticity — equal variance) - Cook’s Distance: Points above 0.5 are influential observations

5 Coefficient Plot

Code 
tidy(model_full, conf.int=TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(term = recode(term,
    edu_years="Education", experience="Experience",
    `I(experience^2)`="Experience²",
    female="Female", urban="Urban", union="Union")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=estimate, y=reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#94a3b8") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=conf.low, xmax=conf.high), height=0.25,
                 color="#7c3aed", linewidth=0.8) +
  geom_point(size=4, color="#2563eb") +
  labs(title="Regression Coefficients with 95% CI",
       x="Coefficient Estimate (₹/unit change)", y=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y=element_blank())

6 Log-Linear Wage Equation (Mincer Model)

A standard approach in labour economics — using log wages gives percentage interpretations:

Code 
model_log <- lm(log_wage ~ edu_years + experience + I(experience^2) + 
                  female + urban + union, data=wage_data)

tidy(model_log) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    term = recode(term,
      edu_years="Education (years)",
      experience="Experience",
      `I(experience^2)`="Experience²",
      female="Female (=1)",
      urban="Urban (=1)",
      union="Union (=1)"),
    pct_effect = round((exp(estimate)-1)*100, 2),
    across(c(estimate, std.error, p.value), round, 4)
  ) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value, pct_effect) |>
  rename(`% Effect` = pct_effect) |>
  kable(caption="Mincer Wage Equation (Log-Linear): % change in wage per unit")
Mincer Wage Equation (Log-Linear): % change in wage per unit
term estimate std.error p.value % Effect
Education (years) 0.0769 0.0031 0 7.99
Experience 0.0372 0.0038 0 3.79
Experience² -0.0029 0.0001 0 -0.29
Female (=1) -0.2593 0.0245 0 -22.84
Urban (=1) 0.1452 0.0241 0 15.62
Union (=1) 0.2042 0.0331 0 22.65

Key finding: The gender wage gap is approximately 22.8% — women earn about this much less than men after controlling for education, experience, and other factors.

7 Practice Exercises

TipTry It Yourself
  1. Add state fixed effects to the model using lm(wage ~ ... + factor(state))
  2. Test for heteroscedasticity using lmtest::bptest()
  3. Compute robust standard errors using sandwich::vcovHC()
  4. Interpret the experience² coefficient — at what experience level do wages peak?

Next: Chapter 4 — Time Series Analysis →

item.title: Chapter 2: Probability & Distributions
item.dc:creator: Pawan Jangra
item.description:
Learning Objectives: Understand core probability concepts, work with common distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, t), and apply these in real-world contexts using R’s built-in distribution functions.

1 Probability Fundamentals

Every distribution in R has four functions:

Prefix Function Returns
d dnorm(x) Probability density at x
p pnorm(q) P(X ≤ q) — CDF
q qnorm(p) Quantile (inverse CDF)
r rnorm(n) n random samples
Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

2 The Normal Distribution

The Normal distribution is the cornerstone of statistical inference — thanks to the Central Limit Theorem.

Code 
# Compare Normal distributions with different parameters
x_vals <- seq(-5, 15, length.out = 500)

normal_df <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(x=x_vals, density=dnorm(x_vals, mean=0, sd=1),   label="N(0,1)"),
  data.frame(x=x_vals, density=dnorm(x_vals, mean=3, sd=1.5), label="N(3,1.5)"),
  data.frame(x=x_vals, density=dnorm(x_vals, mean=5, sd=0.5), label="N(5,0.5)"),
  data.frame(x=x_vals, density=dnorm(x_vals, mean=2, sd=2.5), label="N(2,2.5)")
)

ggplot(normal_df, aes(x=x, y=density, color=label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#2563eb","#7c3aed","#0d9488","#d97706")) +
  labs(title="Normal Distributions with Different Parameters",
       subtitle="Location (μ) shifts the center; Scale (σ) controls spread",
       x="x", y="Density", color="Distribution") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="right", panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

2.1 The 68-95-99.7 Rule

Code 
x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out=1000)
df_norm <- data.frame(x=x, y=dnorm(x))

ggplot(df_norm, aes(x,y)) +
  geom_area(data=filter(df_norm, x >= -3 & x <= 3), fill="#93c5fd", alpha=0.3) +
  geom_area(data=filter(df_norm, x >= -2 & x <= 2), fill="#60a5fa", alpha=0.4) +
  geom_area(data=filter(df_norm, x >= -1 & x <= 1), fill="#2563eb", alpha=0.5) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.5, color="#1e3a8a") +
  geom_vline(xintercept=c(-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3), linetype="dashed", 
             alpha=0.4, color="#64748b") +
  annotate("text", x=0, y=0.2, label="68%\n(±1σ)", size=3.5, fontface="bold", color="white") +
  annotate("text", x=1.7, y=0.05, label="95%\n(±2σ)", size=3, color="#1e40af") +
  annotate("text", x=2.7, y=0.015, label="99.7%\n(±3σ)", size=2.5, color="#1e3a8a") +
  labs(title="Empirical Rule: 68-95-99.7",
       x="Standard Deviations from Mean", y="Density") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())
Code 
# Probability calculations
cat("P(Z ≤ 1.96)  =", round(pnorm(1.96), 4), "\n")
P(Z ≤ 1.96)  = 0.975
Code 
cat("P(-1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) =", round(pnorm(1.96) - pnorm(-1.96), 4), "\n")
P(-1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.95
Code 
cat("P(Z > 2.33)  =", round(1 - pnorm(2.33), 4), "\n")
P(Z > 2.33)  = 0.0099
Code 
cat("95th percentile (z):", round(qnorm(0.95), 4), "\n")
95th percentile (z): 1.6449
Code 
cat("99th percentile (z):", round(qnorm(0.99), 4), "\n")
99th percentile (z): 2.3263

3 Binomial Distribution

Used when we have n independent trials, each with probability p of success.

Application: In a survey of 100 households, if 35% own agricultural land, what’s the probability that exactly 40 own land?

Code 
n_trials <- 100
p_success <- 0.35

binom_df <- data.frame(
  k = 0:n_trials,
  prob = dbinom(0:n_trials, n_trials, p_success)
) |> filter(prob > 0.0001)

# Highlight specific outcomes
binom_df <- binom_df |>
  mutate(highlight = case_when(
    k == 40 ~ "k = 40",
    k >= 35 & k <= 40 ~ "35-40",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))

ggplot(binom_df, aes(x=k, y=prob, fill=highlight)) +
  geom_col(width=0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("k = 40"="#e11d48","35-40"="#7c3aed","Other"="#93c5fd"),
                    name="") +
  labs(title=paste0("Binomial(n=", n_trials, ", p=", p_success, ")"),
       x="Number of successes (k)", y="Probability P(X = k)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="top", panel.grid.minor=element_blank())
Code 
cat("P(X = 40) =", round(dbinom(40, 100, 0.35), 4))
P(X = 40) = 0.0474
Code 
cat("\nP(X ≥ 40) =", round(1 - pbinom(39, 100, 0.35), 4))

P(X ≥ 40) = 0.1724
Code 
cat("\nExpected value E(X) = np =", 100*0.35)

Expected value E(X) = np = 35
Code 
cat("\nSD(X) = √(np(1-p)) =", round(sqrt(100*0.35*0.65), 3))

SD(X) = √(np(1-p)) = 4.77

4 Poisson Distribution

Used for count data — events happening in a fixed interval.

Application: If a field surveyor visits an average of 8 households per hour, what’s the probability of visiting exactly 10 in an hour?

Code 
lambdas <- c(1, 3, 8, 15)
pois_df <- expand.grid(
  k = 0:30,
  lambda = lambdas
) |>
  mutate(
    prob  = dpois(k, lambda),
    label = paste0("λ = ", lambda)
  ) |> filter(prob > 0.001)

ggplot(pois_df, aes(x=k, y=prob, color=label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.1) +
  geom_point(size=2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#2563eb","#7c3aed","#0d9488","#d97706")) +
  labs(title="Poisson Distributions",
       x="k (count)", y="P(X = k)", color="Parameter") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="right", panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

5 The t-Distribution

Used when the population standard deviation is unknown (which is almost always in practice).

Code 
x <- seq(-5, 5, length.out=500)
t_df <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(x=x, y=dnorm(x), dist="Normal (Z)"),
  data.frame(x=x, y=dt(x, df=3),  dist="t(df=3)"),
  data.frame(x=x, y=dt(x, df=10), dist="t(df=10)"),
  data.frame(x=x, y=dt(x, df=30), dist="t(df=30)")
)

ggplot(t_df, aes(x, y, color=dist, linetype=dist)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#0f172a","#e11d48","#7c3aed","#2563eb")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values=c("dashed","solid","solid","solid")) +
  labs(title="t-Distribution vs Normal",
       subtitle="As df increases, t approaches the Standard Normal",
       x="x", y="Density", color="Distribution", linetype="Distribution") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="right", panel.grid.minor=element_blank())
Code 
# Critical values
cat("t-critical values (two-tailed, α=0.05):\n")
t-critical values (two-tailed, α=0.05):
Code 
for (df in c(5, 10, 20, 30, 60, 120)) {
  cat(sprintf("  df=%3d: t = %.4f\n", df, qt(0.975, df)))
}
  df=  5: t = 2.5706
  df= 10: t = 2.2281
  df= 20: t = 2.0860
  df= 30: t = 2.0423
  df= 60: t = 2.0003
  df=120: t = 1.9799
Code 
cat(sprintf("  Normal: z = %.4f\n", qnorm(0.975)))
  Normal: z = 1.9600

6 Practice Exercises

TipTry It Yourself
  1. Normal: If IQ scores are N(100, 15²), find P(IQ > 130) and the 90th percentile
  2. Binomial: In 200 trials with p=0.25, find the mean, SD, and P(X ≥ 60)
  3. Poisson: If λ=5 calls/hour, find P(0 calls) and P(X ≥ 8)
  4. t: For a sample of n=25, find the 95% CI for the mean wage from Chapter 1’s data

Next: Chapter 3 — Regression Analysis →

item.title: Chapter 1: Descriptive Statistics
item.dc:creator: Pawan Jangra
item.description:
Learning Objectives: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to compute and interpret measures of central tendency and dispersion, create effective visualizations for exploring distributions, and identify skewness and outliers in real datasets.

1 Introduction to Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics summarize and describe the main features of a dataset. They are the first step in any data analysis — before we run a single regression or hypothesis test, we must understand what our data looks like.

In R, we have powerful tools for this. Let’s load our packages and create a realistic dataset:

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Simulate Labour Force Survey data
set.seed(2025)
n <- 1000

lfs_data <- data.frame(
  age       = round(rnorm(n, 35, 12)) |> pmax(15) |> pmin(70),
  wage      = exp(rnorm(n, log(400), 0.7)) |> round(),
  edu_years = sample(0:20, n, replace = TRUE, 
                     prob = dpois(0:20, 8) + 0.01),
  gender    = sample(c("Male","Female"), n, replace = TRUE, prob=c(.6,.4)),
  state     = sample(c("Haryana","UP","Bihar","Maharashtra","Karnataka"), 
                     n, replace = TRUE),
  sector    = sample(c("Agriculture","Industry","Services"), n, 
                     replace = TRUE, prob = c(.4,.2,.4))
)

glimpse(lfs_data)
Rows: 1,000
Columns: 6
$ age       <dbl> 42, 35, 44, 50, 39, 33, 40, 34, 31, 43, 30, 15, 30, 44, 48, …
$ wage      <dbl> 737, 93, 261, 148, 239, 242, 229, 505, 385, 337, 256, 148, 2…
$ edu_years <int> 2, 4, 4, 2, 9, 8, 16, 11, 5, 4, 7, 10, 4, 9, 10, 6, 10, 13, …
$ gender    <chr> "Male", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Femal…
$ state     <chr> "UP", "Maharashtra", "Haryana", "UP", "Karnataka", "Bihar", …
$ sector    <chr> "Services", "Agriculture", "Services", "Industry", "Industry…

2 Measures of Central Tendency

The three main measures of central tendency each tell a different story:

Code 
wage_stats <- lfs_data |>
  summarise(
    Mean   = mean(wage),
    Median = median(wage),
    Mode   = as.numeric(names(sort(table(round(wage,-2)), decreasing=TRUE)[1])),
    SD     = sd(wage),
    IQR    = IQR(wage),
    Min    = min(wage),
    Max    = max(wage),
    Skewness = (mean(wage) - median(wage)) / sd(wage) * 3  # Pearson's approx
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Statistic", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(Value = round(Value, 2))

kable(wage_stats, caption = "Descriptive Statistics for Daily Wage (₹)")
Descriptive Statistics for Daily Wage (₹)
Statistic Value
Mean 505.76
Median 394.00
Mode 200.00
SD 444.35
IQR 397.25
Min 46.00
Max 6001.00
Skewness 0.75

2.1 Why the Mean ≠ Median for Wages

Code 
ggplot(lfs_data, aes(x = wage)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#93c5fd", alpha = 0.8, color = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(wage), linetype = "Mean"), 
             color = "#2563eb", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(wage), linetype = "Median"), 
             color = "#e11d48", linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = "", values = c("Mean"="solid","Median"="dashed")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(prefix = "₹")) +
  labs(title = "Daily Wage Distribution",
       subtitle = "Right skew pulls the mean above the median",
       x = "Daily Wage (₹)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

Key insight: For right-skewed income distributions, the median is almost always a better measure of “typical” than the mean. A few high earners inflate the mean.

3 Measures of Dispersion

Code 
# Boxplot comparison across sectors
ggplot(lfs_data, aes(x = sector, y = wage, fill = sector)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8, outlier.shape = 21, outlier.alpha = 0.5,
               outlier.size = 1.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23, 
               size = 4, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2563eb","#7c3aed","#0d9488")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(prefix = "₹")) +
  labs(title = "Wage Dispersion by Sector",
       subtitle = "Diamonds = means · Boxes = IQR · Whiskers = 1.5×IQR",
       x = NULL, y = "Daily Wage (₹)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
Code 
dispersion_by_sector <- lfs_data |>
  group_by(sector) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    Mean   = round(mean(wage)),
    SD     = round(sd(wage)),
    `CV (%)` = round(sd(wage)/mean(wage)*100, 1),
    Median = round(median(wage)),
    IQR    = round(IQR(wage)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(dispersion_by_sector, 
      caption = "Dispersion measures by sector (CV = Coefficient of Variation)")
Dispersion measures by sector (CV = Coefficient of Variation)
sector n Mean SD CV (%) Median IQR
Agriculture 423 499 406 81.4 402 384
Industry 214 485 400 82.3 383 326
Services 363 526 508 96.6 386 440

Coefficient of Variation (CV): Useful for comparing dispersion across groups with different means. CV = (SD / Mean) × 100%. Higher CV means more relative spread.

4 Exploring Distributions

4.1 Five-Number Summary

Code 
five_num <- lfs_data |>
  group_by(gender) |>
  summarise(
    Min    = min(wage),
    Q1     = quantile(wage, 0.25),
    Median = median(wage),
    Q3     = quantile(wage, 0.75),
    Max    = max(wage),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(five_num, digits = 0, 
      caption = "Five-number summary by gender (₹)")
Five-number summary by gender (₹)
gender Min Q1 Median Q3 Max
Female 53 225 395 577 6001
Male 46 241 391 667 4098

4.2 Detecting Outliers

Code 
# IQR method for outlier detection
wage_bounds <- lfs_data |>
  summarise(
    Q1  = quantile(wage, 0.25),
    Q3  = quantile(wage, 0.75),
    IQR = IQR(wage),
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR,
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
  )

lfs_outliers <- lfs_data |>
  mutate(is_outlier = wage < wage_bounds$lower | wage > wage_bounds$upper)

cat("Outliers detected (IQR method):", sum(lfs_outliers$is_outlier), "\n")
Outliers detected (IQR method): 54
Code 
cat("That's", round(mean(lfs_outliers$is_outlier)*100, 1), "% of observations\n")
That's 5.4 % of observations

5 Cross-tabulation: Two-Way Frequencies

Code 
# Sector by state distribution
cross_tab <- lfs_data |>
  count(sector, gender) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(Total = Male + Female,
         `Female Share (%)` = round(Female/Total*100, 1))

kable(cross_tab, caption = "Employment distribution by sector and gender")
Employment distribution by sector and gender
sector Female Male Total Female Share (%)
Agriculture 189 234 423 44.7
Industry 94 120 214 43.9
Services 143 220 363 39.4

6 Practice Exercises

TipTry It Yourself
  1. Compute the Gini coefficient for wage inequality using ineq package
  2. Create a violin plot comparing wages across all 5 states
  3. Test whether mean wages differ significantly between sectors using ANOVA
  4. Compute the 90th percentile wage separately for male and female workers

7 Chapter Summary

Concept Measure R Function
Central Tendency Mean mean()
Central Tendency Median median()
Dispersion Standard Deviation sd()
Dispersion IQR IQR()
Shape Skewness moments::skewness()
Range Min/Max min(), max()
Quantiles Quartiles quantile()

Next: Chapter 2 — Probability & Distributions →
